title: Median of two sorted arrays
description: Find the median of two sorted arrays efficiently

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize merged=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=2, 1<=2, so append 1 to merged: merged=[1], i=1. Step 3: Compare nums1[1]=3 and nums2[0]=2, 3>2, so append 2 to merged: merged=[1,2], j=1. Step 4: nums2 exhausted, append remaining nums1[1:]=[3] to merged: merged=[1,2,3]. Step 5: n=3 (odd), median=merged[3//2]=merged[1]=2.0.

Step 1: Initialize merged=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=3, 1<=3, so append 1 to merged: merged=[1], i=1. Step 3: Compare nums1[1]=2 and nums2[0]=3, 2<=3, so append 2 to merged: merged=[1,2], i=2. Step 4: nums1 exhausted, append remaining nums2[0:]=[3,4] to merged: merged=[1,2,3,4]. Step 5: n=4 (even), median=(merged[4//2-1]+merged[4//2])/2=(merged[1]+merged[2])/2=(2+3)/2=2.5.

approaches.name: Brute Force Merge O(m+n)
approaches.summary: Merge the two sorted arrays into one sorted array, then find the median by accessing the middle element(s). Use this for simplicity when input sizes are small.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Median of Two Sorted Arrays via Brute Force Merge
ladders.desc: Complete solution: merge both arrays into one sorted array, then find median.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the median problem by first combining both sorted arrays into a single sorted array, then directly computing the median from that merged array. The intuition is straightforward: if we have all elements in sorted order, finding the median becomes trivial—we just need to access the middle element(s). This works because merging preserves the sorted property: we compare elements from both arrays sequentially, always picking the smaller one to add to the merged array. This ensures the final array is sorted. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty merged array and two pointers at the start of each input array. 2) While both pointers are within bounds, compare elements at pointers, append the smaller to merged array, and move that pointer forward. 3) After one array is exhausted, append all remaining elements from the other array. 4) Compute median: if merged length is odd, return middle element; if even, return average of two middle elements. Time complexity is O(m+n) where m and n are array lengths, as we process each element once. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the merged array. This approach is simple and reliable for small inputs but inefficient for large ones. Connection to L1: This level implements the complete solution using the merge subroutine from L1. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty arrays, incorrect median calculation for even lengths (must average two middle elements), and off-by-one errors in pointer movement. Real code pattern: def findMedianSortedArrays(nums1, nums2): merged = []; i=j=0; while i<len(nums1) and j<len(nums2): if nums1[i]<=nums2[j]: merged.append(nums1[i]); i+=1 else: merged.append(nums2[j]); j+=1; merged.extend(nums1[i:]); merged.extend(nums2[j:]); n=len(merged); if n%2==1: return merged[n//2]; else: return (merged[n//2-1]+merged[n//2])/2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize merged=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=2, 1<=2, so append 1 to merged: merged=[1], i=1. Step 3: Compare nums1[1]=3 and nums2[0]=2, 3>2, so append 2 to merged: merged=[1,2], j=1. Step 4: nums2 exhausted, append remaining nums1[1:]=[3] to merged: merged=[1,2,3]. Step 5: n=3 (odd), median=merged[3//2]=merged[1]=2.0.

Step 1: Initialize merged=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=3, 1<=3, so append 1 to merged: merged=[1], i=1. Step 3: Compare nums1[1]=2 and nums2[0]=3, 2<=3, so append 2 to merged: merged=[1,2], i=2. Step 4: nums1 exhausted, append remaining nums2[0:]=[3,4] to merged: merged=[1,2,3,4]. Step 5: n=4 (even), median=(merged[4//2-1]+merged[4//2])/2=(merged[1]+merged[2])/2=(2+3)/2=2.5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Merge two sorted arrays into one sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach: it takes two sorted arrays and combines them into a single sorted array. The intuition is based on the merge step from merge sort: since both arrays are already sorted, we can efficiently merge them by always picking the smallest available element. This works because comparing the current elements from each array guarantees we maintain sorted order in the output. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty result array and two pointers i and j at 0. 2) While i < len(nums1) and j < len(nums2): compare nums1[i] and nums2[j]. If nums1[i] <= nums2[j], append nums1[i] to result and increment i; else append nums2[j] and increment j. 3) After the loop, one array may have remaining elements; append all of them to result. Time complexity is O(m+n) as each element is processed once. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the result array. Connection to L0: This merge subroutine is directly used in L0 to create the merged array before median computation. Common mistakes include using < instead of <= in comparison (which can affect stability but not correctness for this problem), forgetting to increment pointers, and not handling remaining elements. Real code pattern: def merge(nums1, nums2): result=[]; i=j=0; while i<len(nums1) and j<len(nums2): if nums1[i]<=nums2[j]: result.append(nums1[i]); i+=1 else: result.append(nums2[j]); j+=1; result.extend(nums1[i:]); result.extend(nums2[j:]); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: result=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: Compare 1 and 2, 1<=2, append 1: result=[1], i=1. Step 3: Compare 3 and 2, 3>2, append 2: result=[1,2], j=1. Step 4: Compare 3 and 4, 3<=4, append 3: result=[1,2,3], i=2. Step 5: Compare 5 and 4, 5>4, append 4: result=[1,2,3,4], j=2. Step 6: nums2 exhausted, append remaining nums1[2:]=[5]: result=[1,2,3,4,5].

Step 1: result=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: Compare 10 and 15, 10<=15, append 10: result=[10], i=1. Step 3: Compare 20 and 15, 20>15, append 15: result=[10,15], j=1. Step 4: Compare 20 and 25, 20<=25, append 20: result=[10,15,20], i=2. Step 5: nums1 exhausted, append remaining nums2[1:]=[25,30]: result=[10,15,20,25,30].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential Comparison and Selection
ladders.desc: The technique of comparing elements from two sorted sequences to build a merged sequence.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the fundamental technique used in merging: sequentially comparing the current elements from two sorted arrays and selecting the smaller one to add to the result. The intuition is that since both arrays are sorted, the smallest overall element must be at one of the two current positions. By repeatedly picking the smaller current element, we ensure the merged sequence remains sorted. This works because each selection reduces the problem size and maintains the invariant that all elements added so far are in order. The algorithm: maintain two pointers, compare elements at those pointers, select the smaller, move that pointer forward, and repeat. Time complexity per comparison is O(1), and overall it scales linearly with total elements. Space complexity depends on storage but conceptually uses O(1) extra space for pointers. Connection to L1: This logic is the heart of the merge subroutine in L1. Common mistakes include comparing without moving pointers (leading to infinite loops), incorrect comparison order (e.g., always picking from first array), and not handling ties properly (using < instead of <= might skip equal elements). Real code pattern: while i < len(nums1) and j < len(nums2): if nums1[i] <= nums2[j]: select nums1[i]; i+=1 else: select nums2[j]; j+=1.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0, compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=2, 1<=2, select 1, move i to 1. Step 2: i=1, j=0, compare nums1[1]=4 and nums2[0]=2, 4>2, select 2, move j to 1. Step 3: i=1, j=1, compare nums1[1]=4 and nums2[1]=3, 4>3, select 3, move j to 2. Step 4: nums2 exhausted, select remaining nums1[1]=4. Final sequence: 1,2,3,4.

Step 1: i=0, j=0, compare 5 and 6, 5<=6, select 5, move i to 1. Step 2: i=1, j=0, compare 10 and 6, 10>6, select 6, move j to 1. Step 3: i=1, j=1, compare 10 and 8, 10>8, select 8, move j to 2. Step 4: nums2 exhausted, select remaining nums1[1]=10. Final sequence: 5,6,8,10.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Using two indices to traverse two arrays simultaneously.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the technique of using two pointers (or indices) to iterate through two arrays at the same time. The intuition is that by maintaining separate positions in each array, we can compare and process elements from both without mixing them up. This is essential for merging because it allows us to access the current smallest elements from each array efficiently. The algorithm: initialize pointer i for nums1 and j for nums2 at 0. In a loop, check if both pointers are within bounds (i < len(nums1) and j < len(nums2)). If so, we can compare nums1[i] and nums2[j]; after processing, increment the pointer corresponding to the selected element. Time complexity per iteration is O(1), and the loop runs until one pointer goes out of bounds. Space complexity is O(1) for the pointers. Connection to L2: This pointer management enables the sequential comparison in L2. Common mistakes include not updating pointers correctly (e.g., incrementing both), going out of bounds by not checking conditions, and using the wrong pointer after the loop. Real code pattern: i=0; j=0; while i < len(nums1) and j < len(nums2): # access nums1[i] and nums2[j]; then update i or j based on comparison.

examples.input: nums1 = [1,3], nums2 = [2,4], iterate until one pointer exhausts
examples.output: Pointer positions: i=0,j=0 -> i=1,j=0 -> i=1,j=1 -> stop (i=1,j=2)
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0, both in bounds. Step 2: Suppose we compare and select from nums1, increment i to 1. Step 3: i=1, j=0, both in bounds. Step 4: Compare and select from nums2, increment j to 1. Step 5: i=1, j=1, both in bounds. Step 6: Compare and select from nums1, increment i to 2. Now i=2 out of bounds, loop stops.

examples.input: nums1 = [5], nums2 = [1,2], iterate until one pointer exhausts
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0, both in bounds. Step 2: Compare and select from nums2, increment j to 1. Step 3: i=0, j=1, both in bounds. Step 4: Compare and select from nums2, increment j to 2. Now j=2 out of bounds, loop stops.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Numbers
ladders.desc: The fundamental act of comparing two integers to determine their order.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two numbers to decide which is smaller or if they are equal. The intuition is that comparison is the key to maintaining sorted order: by comparing elements, we can decide which one should come first in a sequence. This works because numbers have a natural order (e.g., 1 < 2 < 3). The algorithm: given two numbers a and b, evaluate the expression a <= b. If true, a is less than or equal to b, so a should come first (or they are equal and order doesn't matter for merging). If false, b is less than a, so b should come first. Time complexity is O(1), as comparison is a constant-time operation. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is used within the two-pointer iteration in L3 to decide which element to select. Common mistakes include using < instead of <= (which can affect handling of equal elements), comparing non-numeric types without proper conversion, and incorrect operator precedence. Real code pattern: if a <= b: # a is smaller or equal; else: # b is smaller.

examples.output: false (5 <= 3 is false, so b is smaller)
examples.trace: Step 1: Evaluate 5 <= 3. Step 2: Since 5 is not less than or equal to 3, the result is false. This means b=3 is smaller than a=5.

examples.output: true (2 <= 2 is true, so a is smaller or equal)
examples.trace: Step 1: Evaluate 2 <= 2. Step 2: Since 2 is equal to 2, the result is true. This means a=2 is less than or equal to b=2, so a can come first.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Sorted Array?
ladders.desc: Understanding that an array is sorted if its elements are in non-decreasing order.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept defines what it means for an array to be sorted: the elements are arranged in non-decreasing order, meaning each element is less than or equal to the next element. The intuition is that sorting organizes data so that finding elements, merging, or computing statistics like median becomes easier. This works because it imposes a structure: for any index i, array[i] <= array[i+1]. The algorithm to check if an array is sorted: iterate through the array from index 0 to len(array)-2, and for each i, check if array[i] <= array[i+1]. If all checks pass, the array is sorted. Time complexity is O(n) for an array of length n. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L4: Sorted arrays enable efficient comparisons in L4, as we can rely on the order when merging. Common mistakes include confusing non-decreasing (allows equals) with strictly increasing (no equals), not handling empty or single-element arrays (which are trivially sorted), and off-by-one errors in the loop. Real code pattern: def is_sorted(arr): for i in range(len(arr)-1): if arr[i] > arr[i+1]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check i=0: arr[0]=1 <= arr[1]=3, true. Step 2: Check i=1: arr[1]=3 <= arr[2]=5, true. All checks pass, so array is sorted.
Step 1: Check i=0: arr[0]=5 <= arr[1]=2, false (5 > 2). Since a check fails, array is not sorted.

approaches.name: Binary Search Partition O(log(min(m,n)))
approaches.summary: Use binary search on the smaller array to partition both arrays such that elements on the left are less than or equal to those on the right, then compute the median from the partition boundaries. This is the most efficient standard approach.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Binary Search Partition for Median of Two Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Complete solution using binary search on the smaller array to partition both arrays and compute the median.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to find a partition point in the smaller array such that all elements on the left side of both arrays are less than or equal to all elements on the right side. This ensures we can compute the median directly from the boundary elements. We perform binary search on the smaller array to find this partition efficiently. The key is to maintain the invariant: maxLeftX <= minRightY and maxLeftY <= minRightX, where X is the smaller array and Y is the larger array. If this condition holds, we've found the correct partition. The median is then computed based on whether the total number of elements is even or odd. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Ensure nums1 is the smaller array by swapping if necessary. 2. Set low = 0, high = len(nums1). 3. While low <= high: a. Compute partitionX = (low + high) // 2, partitionY = (m + n + 1) // 2 - partitionX. b. Get maxLeftX, minRightX, maxLeftY, minRightY (use -inf/inf for boundaries). c. If maxLeftX <= minRightY and maxLeftY <= minRightX: i. If total length odd: return max(maxLeftX, maxLeftY). ii. Else: return (max(maxLeftX, maxLeftY) + min(minRightX, minRightY)) / 2. d. Else if maxLeftX > minRightY: high = partitionX - 1. e. Else: low = partitionX + 1. Time complexity: O(log(min(m,n))) due to binary search on the smaller array. Space complexity: O(1) as we use only constant extra space. Connection to L1: This level integrates the partition validation logic from L1 into the full binary search framework. Common mistakes: Not handling edge cases like empty arrays or arrays of different sizes, incorrect boundary conditions for partition indices, and forgetting to handle both odd and even total lengths. Real code pattern: def findMedianSortedArrays(nums1, nums2): if len(nums1) > len(nums2): nums1, nums2 = nums2, nums1 m, n = len(nums1), len(nums2) low, high = 0, m while low <= high: partitionX = (low + high) // 2 partitionY = (m + n + 1) // 2 - partitionX maxLeftX = float('-inf') if partitionX == 0 else nums1[partitionX-1] minRightX = float('inf') if partitionX == m else nums1[partitionX] maxLeftY = float('-inf') if partitionY == 0 else nums2[partitionY-1] minRightY = float('inf') if partitionY == n else nums2[partitionY] if maxLeftX <= minRightY and maxLeftY <= minRightX: if (m + n) % 2 == 1: return max(maxLeftX, maxLeftY) else: return (max(maxLeftX, maxLeftY) + min(minRightX, minRightY)) / 2 elif maxLeftX > minRightY: high = partitionX - 1 else: low = partitionX + 1

examples.trace: Step 1: Ensure nums1 is smaller: nums1=[1,3] (m=2), nums2=[2] (n=1), already true. Step 2: low=0, high=2. Step 3: Binary search iteration 1: partitionX=(0+2)//2=1, partitionY=(2+1+1)//2-1=1. maxLeftX=nums1[0]=1, minRightX=nums1[1]=3, maxLeftY=nums2[0]=2, minRightY=inf (since partitionY=n). Check: maxLeftX=1 <= minRightY=inf ✓, maxLeftY=2 <= minRightX=3 ✓. Total length=3 (odd), median=max(1,2)=2.0. Return 2.0.

Step 1: Ensure nums1 is smaller: nums1=[1,2] (m=2), nums2=[3,4] (n=2), already true. Step 2: low=0, high=2. Step 3: Binary search iteration 1: partitionX=(0+2)//2=1, partitionY=(2+2+1)//2-1=2. maxLeftX=nums1[0]=1, minRightX=nums1[1]=2, maxLeftY=nums2[1]=4, minRightY=inf (since partitionY=n). Check: maxLeftX=1 <= minRightY=inf ✓, maxLeftY=4 <= minRightX=2 ✗. Since maxLeftY > minRightX, low=partitionX+1=2. Step 4: Iteration 2: low=2, high=2, partitionX=(2+2)//2=2, partitionY=(2+2+1)//2-2=1. maxLeftX=nums1[1]=2, minRightX=inf (since partitionX=m), maxLeftY=nums2[0]=3, minRightY=nums2[1]=4. Check: maxLeftX=2 <= minRightY=4 ✓, maxLeftY=3 <= minRightX=inf ✓. Total length=4 (even), median=(max(2,3)+min(inf,4))/2=(3+4)/2=3.5? Wait, recalc: maxLeftX=2, maxLeftY=3, so max=3; minRightX=inf, minRightY=4, so min=4; (3+4)/2=3.5. But expected 2.5. Error: partitionY calculation should be (m+n)//2 - partitionX for even? Let's correct: In algorithm, partitionY = (m+n+1)//2 - partitionX. For m=2,n=2, (2+2+1)//2=2, partitionY=2-2=0. Then maxLeftY=-inf, minRightY=nums2[0]=3. Check: maxLeftX=2 <= minRightY=3 ✓, maxLeftY=-inf <= minRightX=inf ✓. Total even, median=(max(2,-inf)+min(inf,3))/2=(2+3)/2=2.5. Correct.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Validate Partition for Median Condition
ladders.desc: Check if a given partition of two sorted arrays satisfies the median condition.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that for a correct partition, all elements on the left side of both arrays must be less than or equal to all elements on the right side. This ensures the median can be computed from the boundary elements. Given two sorted arrays nums1 and nums2, and partition indices partitionX and partitionY, we define: maxLeftX = maximum element on left of nums1, minRightX = minimum element on right of nums1, similarly for nums2. The condition for a valid partition is: maxLeftX <= minRightY and maxLeftY <= minRightX. If this holds, we have found the correct split. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Compute m = len(nums1), n = len(nums2). 2. Handle edge cases: if partitionX is 0, maxLeftX = -inf; if partitionX is m, minRightX = inf; similarly for partitionY. 3. Check the two inequalities. 4. If true, compute median based on total length parity. Time complexity: O(1) as it involves only a few comparisons and array accesses. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L0: This validation is the core check within the binary search loop in the full problem. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle boundary cases (e.g., when partition is at the start or end of an array), using wrong indices for array access, and not considering both inequalities. Real code pattern: def is_valid_partition(nums1, nums2, partitionX, partitionY): m, n = len(nums1), len(nums2) maxLeftX = float('-inf') if partitionX == 0 else nums1[partitionX-1] minRightX = float('inf') if partitionX == m else nums1[partitionX] maxLeftY = float('-inf') if partitionY == 0 else nums2[partitionY-1] minRightY = float('inf') if partitionY == n else nums2[partitionY] return maxLeftX <= minRightY and maxLeftY <= minRightX

examples.trace: Step 1: m=2, n=1. Step 2: partitionX=1, so maxLeftX=nums1[0]=1, minRightX=nums1[1]=3. partitionY=1, so maxLeftY=nums2[0]=2, minRightY=inf (since partitionY=n). Step 3: Check: 1 <= inf ✓, 2 <= 3 ✓. Return True.
Step 1: m=2, n=2. Step 2: partitionX=1, maxLeftX=nums1[0]=1, minRightX=nums1[1]=2. partitionY=2, maxLeftY=nums2[1]=4, minRightY=inf. Step 3: Check: 1 <= inf ✓, 4 <= 2 ✗. Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Partition Index
ladders.desc: Use binary search to find the correct partition index in the smaller array.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that we can treat the partition index in the smaller array as a search space, and use binary search to efficiently find the index that satisfies the median condition. Since the arrays are sorted, adjusting the partition index allows us to control the number of elements on the left and right sides. We start with low=0 and high=len(smaller array), and iteratively adjust based on whether the current partition puts too many elements from the smaller array on the left (making maxLeftX too large) or too few (making maxLeftY too large). Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Let nums1 be the smaller array (swap if needed). 2. Initialize low=0, high=len(nums1). 3. While low <= high: a. Compute mid = (low + high) // 2 as partitionX. b. Compute partitionY = (total_length + 1) // 2 - partitionX. c. Check validation condition (from L1). d. If valid, break; else if maxLeftX > minRightY, move high = mid - 1; else move low = mid + 1. Time complexity: O(log(min(m,n))) due to binary search. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L1: This level uses the validation from L1 to guide the binary search decisions. Common mistakes: Incorrect calculation of partitionY, not updating low/high correctly, and infinite loops if termination condition is wrong. Real code pattern: def binary_search_partition(nums1, nums2): if len(nums1) > len(nums2): nums1, nums2 = nums2, nums1 m, n = len(nums1), len(nums2) low, high = 0, m while low <= high: partitionX = (low + high) // 2 partitionY = (m + n + 1) // 2 - partitionX # validation logic here if valid: return partitionX, partitionY elif maxLeftX > minRightY: high = partitionX - 1 else: low = partitionX + 1

examples.trace: Step 1: nums1 is smaller, m=2, n=1. Step 2: low=0, high=2. Step 3: Iteration 1: partitionX=(0+2)//2=1, partitionY=(2+1+1)//2-1=1. Validate: maxLeftX=1, minRightX=3, maxLeftY=2, minRightY=inf → valid. Return (1,1).

Step 1: nums1 is smaller, m=2, n=2. Step 2: low=0, high=2. Step 3: Iteration 1: partitionX=1, partitionY=(2+2+1)//2-1=2. Validate: maxLeftX=1, minRightX=2, maxLeftY=4, minRightY=inf → invalid (4>2), so low=2. Step 4: Iteration 2: low=2, high=2, partitionX=2, partitionY=(4+1)//2-2=0. Validate: maxLeftX=2, minRightX=inf, maxLeftY=-inf, minRightY=3 → valid. Return (2,0).

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Median from Partition Boundaries
ladders.desc: Calculate the median given the boundary elements of a valid partition.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that once we have a valid partition, the median is determined by the maximum of the left elements and the minimum of the right elements. For an odd total number of elements, the median is simply the maximum of the left elements because the partition is set to have one more element on the left. For an even total, the median is the average of the maximum left and minimum right elements. Given maxLeftX, maxLeftY, minRightX, minRightY from a valid partition, we compute: If total length is odd: median = max(maxLeftX, maxLeftY). If even: median = (max(maxLeftX, maxLeftY) + min(minRightX, minRightY)) / 2. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Compute total_length = m + n. 2. If total_length % 2 == 1: return max(maxLeftX, maxLeftY). 3. Else: return (max(maxLeftX, maxLeftY) + min(minRightX, minRightY)) / 2. Time complexity: O(1). Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L2: This computation is performed after binary search finds a valid partition. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle both odd and even cases, using wrong values for max/min, and not converting to float for division in even case. Real code pattern: def compute_median(maxLeftX, maxLeftY, minRightX, minRightY, total_length): if total_length % 2 == 1: return max(maxLeftX, maxLeftY) else: return (max(maxLeftX, maxLeftY) + min(minRightX, minRightY)) / 2.0

examples.trace: Step 1: total_length=3 (odd). Step 2: median = max(1,2) = 2.0.
Step 1: total_length=4 (even). Step 2: max_left = max(2, -inf) = 2. min_right = min(inf, 3) = 3. median = (2 + 3) / 2 = 2.5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Array Elements with Boundary Handling
ladders.desc: Safely access elements in an array given an index, handling cases where the index is out of bounds.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that when partitioning arrays, we may need to access elements at the boundaries (e.g., before the first element or after the last element). To handle this, we use sentinel values: -inf for elements before the start and inf for elements after the end. This allows comparisons to work correctly without explicit bounds checking in the median condition. Given an array arr and an index i, we want to get the element at position i-1 (for left side) or i (for right side), but if i is 0, there is no left element, so we return -inf; if i is len(arr), there is no right element, so we return inf. Step-by-step algorithm: For left element: if index == 0: return -inf else: return arr[index-1]. For right element: if index == len(arr): return inf else: return arr[index]. Time complexity: O(1). Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used to compute maxLeftX, minRightX, etc., in the median computation. Common mistakes: Using wrong indices (off-by-one errors), not handling edge cases, and using incorrect sentinel values. Real code pattern: def get_left_element(arr, index): if index == 0: return float('-inf') else: return arr[index-1] def get_right_element(arr, index): if index == len(arr): return float('inf') else: return arr[index]

examples.trace: Step 1: For left element: index=2 != 0, so arr[1]=3. Step 2: For right element: index=2 != len(arr)=3, so arr[2]=5.
Step 1: For left element: index=0, so return -inf. Step 2: For right element: index=0 != len(arr)=2, so arr[0]=10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Median of a Single Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Find the median of one sorted array, understanding odd vs. even length cases.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that the median is the middle value that divides a dataset into two equal halves. For a sorted array, if the length is odd, the median is the middle element; if even, it's the average of the two middle elements. This foundational concept is crucial because the median of two arrays extends this idea to combined datasets. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Let n = len(arr). 2. If n % 2 == 1: median = arr[n//2]. 3. Else: median = (arr[n//2 - 1] + arr[n//2]) / 2. Time complexity: O(1) for access. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L4: This is a simpler case without boundary handling, but introduces the parity logic used in higher levels. Common mistakes: Using wrong indices for array access, not converting to float for even case, and misunderstanding median definition. Real code pattern: def median_single_array(arr): n = len(arr) if n % 2 == 1: return arr[n//2] else: return (arr[n//2 - 1] + arr[n//2]) / 2.0

examples.trace: Step 1: n=3 (odd). Step 2: median = arr[3//2] = arr[1] = 2.0.

approaches.name: Two Pointer Linear O(m+n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers to traverse both arrays simultaneously to find the median without merging the entire arrays. Suitable when logarithmic complexity is not required but space must be constant.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Median of Two Sorted Arrays using Two Pointer Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Find the median of two sorted arrays by traversing them simultaneously with two pointers without merging.
ladders.explanation: The median of two sorted arrays can be found by conceptually merging them in sorted order, but we don't need to actually create the merged array. Since the arrays are sorted, we can use two pointers (i for nums1, j for nums2) to traverse both arrays in order, counting elements until we reach the median position(s). For total length n = m + n, if n is odd, the median is the element at position n//2 (0-indexed) in the merged order; if n is even, it's the average of elements at positions n//2 - 1 and n//2. We maintain current and previous variables to track the last two elements seen during traversal. The intuition is that by moving the pointer pointing to the smaller current element, we simulate the merged order. We stop when we've processed enough elements to reach the median position(s). Time complexity is O(m+n) as we may traverse all elements, and space is O(1) using only a few variables. This approach is straightforward and avoids extra space, making it suitable when logarithmic complexity isn't required but constant space is preferred. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in counting, mishandling empty arrays, and incorrect median calculation for even lengths. Always validate inputs and edge cases like single-element arrays or arrays with overlapping ranges.

examples.trace: Total length n = 3 (odd). Median position = n//2 = 1 (0-indexed). Initialize i=0, j=0, count=0, prev=0, curr=0. While count <= 1: count=0: i=0 (1), j=0 (2). 1 < 2, so prev=curr=0, curr=1, i=1, count=1. count=1: i=1 (3), j=0 (2). 2 < 3, so prev=curr=1, curr=2, j=1, count=2 (stop as count > 1). Median = curr = 2.0.

Total length n = 4 (even). Median positions = n//2 - 1 = 1 and n//2 = 2 (0-indexed). Initialize i=0, j=0, count=0, prev=0, curr=0. While count <= 2: count=0: i=0 (1), j=0 (3). 1 < 3, prev=curr=0, curr=1, i=1, count=1. count=1: i=1 (2), j=0 (3). 2 < 3, prev=curr=1, curr=2, i=2, count=2. count=2: i=2 (out), j=0 (3). Since i out, take from nums2: prev=curr=2, curr=3, j=1, count=3 (stop as count > 2). Median = (prev + curr) / 2 = (2 + 3) / 2 = 2.5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Simulating Merged Order Traversal with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Traverse two sorted arrays in sorted order using two pointers to find the k-th smallest element.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine finds the k-th smallest element (0-indexed) in the merged order of two sorted arrays without actually merging them. Use pointers i and j for nums1 and nums2. Compare nums1[i] and nums2[j]; the smaller one is the next element in merged order. Move the pointer of the smaller element forward and increment a count. Repeat until count reaches k. The intuition leverages the sorted property: at any step, the next smallest overall must be the smaller of the current elements pointed to, as all previous elements are already processed. This simulates a merge sort merge step but stops early. Time complexity is O(k), which in worst case is O(m+n), and space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the core mechanism to locate median positions. Common mistakes include not handling when one array is exhausted, leading to index errors. Always check bounds before accessing. Pseudocode: initialize i=0, j=0, count=0; while count < k: if i < len(nums1) and (j >= len(nums2) or nums1[i] < nums2[j]): curr = nums1[i]; i++; else: curr = nums2[j]; j++; count++; return curr. This pattern is reusable for any order statistic.

examples.trace: Find 2nd smallest (0-indexed, so k=2 means third smallest). i=0, j=0, count=0. count=0: nums1[0]=1, nums2[0]=2, 1<2, curr=1, i=1, count=1. count=1: nums1[1]=3, nums2[0]=2, 2<3, curr=2, j=1, count=2. count=2: nums1[1]=3, nums2[1]=4, 3<4, curr=3, i=2, count=3 (stop as count > k). Return 3.

Find 3rd smallest (k=3). i=0, j=0, count=0. count=0: 10<15, curr=10, i=1, count=1. count=1: 20<15? No, 15<20, curr=15, j=1, count=2. count=2: 20<25, curr=20, i=2, count=3. count=3: i out, so from nums2: curr=25, j=2, count=4 (stop). Return 25.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing and Advancing Pointers in Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Determine which pointer to advance by comparing current elements of two sorted arrays.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is to compare nums1[i] and nums2[j] at each step to decide which array contributes the next smallest element to the merged order. If nums1[i] < nums2[j], then nums1[i] is smaller, so advance i; otherwise, advance j. If one array is exhausted, always take from the other. The intuition is that since both arrays are sorted, the current elements are the smallest unprocessed in their respective arrays. Thus, the global minimum among unprocessed elements is the smaller of these two. This comparison drives the traversal order. Time per step is O(1), and overall it's linear in the number of steps. This feeds into L1 by being the decision mechanism in the traversal loop. Common mistakes include using <= instead of <, which can affect order in case of ties (but for median, order doesn't matter as long as consistent). Also, forgetting to check bounds before comparison. Pseudocode: if i < len(nums1) and (j >= len(nums2) or nums1[i] < nums2[j]): take from nums1; else: take from nums2. This pattern is fundamental for any two-pointer merge simulation.

examples.trace: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=2. 1 < 2, so nums1 has the smaller element. Advance i to 1.
Compare nums1[0]=5 and nums2[0]=1. 5 < 1? No, 1 < 5, so nums2 has the smaller element. Advance j to 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating with While Loops and Counters
ladders.desc: Use a while loop with a counter to process elements until a target count is reached.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves using a while loop that continues until a counter reaches a specific value, such as k in finding the k-th element. Initialize count = 0, and in each iteration, perform an operation (e.g., advance a pointer) and increment count. The loop condition is count < target. The intuition is to control the number of iterations precisely, which is essential for stopping early in algorithms like median finding. This feeds into L2 by providing the control flow for the pointer advancement. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors: if target is 0-indexed, use count < target; if 1-indexed, adjust accordingly. Also, ensure the counter is incremented correctly in all branches. Pseudocode: count = 0; while count < target: # do something; count += 1. This pattern is common in many algorithms where you need to process a fixed number of items. Time complexity depends on the loop body, but the loop itself runs O(target) times. Space is O(1) for the counter.

examples.trace: Initialize count=0. While count < 3: count=0, print 1, count=1; count=1, print 2, count=2; count=2, print 3, count=3; loop stops as count not < 3.
Initialize count=0. While count < 0: condition false immediately, so no iterations, output nothing.

testCases.input: target = 5, process: increment a sum starting at 0 by 1 each time
target = 1, process: set a flag to true
target = 2, process: append 'a' to a list
target = 4, process: multiply a value by 2 starting from 1
target = 3, process: decrement a counter from 10
target = 6, process: add index to total starting at 0
target = 2, process: toggle a boolean starting false

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements and Updating Pointers
ladders.desc: Read an element from an array using an index and increment the index to move to the next element.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using an integer index to access an element in an array, e.g., nums[i] returns the value at position i. After processing, update the index, typically by incrementing i += 1 to move to the next element. The intuition is that arrays are contiguous in memory, so incrementing the index linearly traverses the array. This feeds into L3 as the action within the loop. Common mistakes include accessing out-of-bounds indices, leading to errors. Always check if i < len(nums) before access. Pseudocode: if i < len(nums): value = nums[i]; i += 1. Time complexity is O(1) per access, and space is O(1) for the index variable. This operation is basic to all array traversals.

examples.trace: Access nums[0] = 10. Update i to 1.
Access nums[1] = 15. Update i to 2 (now out of bounds, so next access must check).

testCases.expected: error if accessed, but i remains 0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to use indices to access elements.
ladders.explanation: An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element has an index, starting from 0 for the first element, 1 for the second, and so on. To access an element, use the syntax array[index], which returns the value at that position. For example, if arr = [5, 10, 15], then arr[0] = 5, arr[1] = 10, arr[2] = 15. The length of an array is the number of elements, e.g., len(arr) = 3. Valid indices range from 0 to len(arr)-1. Accessing outside this range causes an error. This foundational concept is crucial for L4 as it underpins all array operations. Intuition: think of an array like a row of boxes numbered starting at 0; you can look inside any box by its number. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing or forgetting that the last index is length-1. Always check bounds. Pseudocode: value = array[i] # only if 0 <= i < len(array). This is basic to programming and necessary for any algorithm involving arrays.

examples.trace: Array arr has elements: index 0 = 3, index 1 = 7, index 2 = 2. Access arr[1] returns 7.
Array arr: index 0 = 100, index 1 = 200. Access arr[0] returns 100.
